title_en: Hadith Categories: Sahih, Hasan, and Da'if
title_ar: Hadith Categories: Sahih, Hasan, and Da'if
content_en:
Al-Kifayah fi Ilm al-Riwayah concludes its systematic treatment of hadith transmission with an analysis of how individual characteristics of narrators and chains combine to produce different categories of hadith. The classification of hadiths as sahih (sound), hasan (good), and da'if (weak) — along with numerous sub-categories — is the practical output of the entire methodological apparatus that the book has built.

The Sahih Hadith

A hadith is classified as sahih (sound) when it meets five conditions simultaneously: (1) the chain of transmission is connected — every narrator actually met and heard from the one above him; (2) every narrator in the chain is morally upright (adil); (3) every narrator is technically precise (dabt); (4) the hadith is free from hidden defects (illa); and (5) it is free from irregularity (shudhudh) — meaning it does not contradict a better-established report.

Al-Khatib explains each condition and the scholarly deliberation required to establish it. The question of connected transmission (ittisal al-isnad) requires establishing that each pair of consecutive narrators actually met, not merely lived in the same era. This is why biographical information about narrators' teachers and students is essential — it is not mere historical curiosity but a necessary foundation for hadith authentication.

The conditions of freedom from hidden defects and irregularity are the most technically demanding. An apparently sound hadith may contain a subtle error — a narrator remembered a name incorrectly, or a chain that looks continuous actually has a hidden break — that only a master hadith scholar with encyclopedic knowledge of the literature can detect. Al-Khatib cites examples from the work of al-Bukhari and al-Daraqutni to illustrate how such defects are identified.

The Hasan Hadith

The category of hasan (good) occupies the space between the sahih and the weak. Al-Khatib's treatment of this category anticipates the more famous definition given later by al-Tirmidhi and refined by Ibn al-Salah and Ibn Hajar. A hasan hadith meets most of the conditions of sahih but falls slightly short in one respect — typically in the degree of precision of one or more narrators, who are acceptable but not among the most precise.

He discusses the practical use of hasan hadiths in legal reasoning (istidlal). The majority of jurists accepted hasan hadiths as legal proofs, ranking them below sahih but above weak. Al-Khatib emphasizes that the distinction between sahih and hasan is a matter of degree, not kind, and that both categories can serve as the basis for legal rulings and theological conclusions.

The Da'if Hadith and Its Uses

Weak (da'if) hadiths are those that fail to meet one or more of the conditions of sahih. Al-Khatib discusses the many varieties of weakness: the broken chain (inqita'), the unknown narrator (majhul), the narrator accused of lying (muttaham bi al-kadhib), the solitary report that contradicts stronger reports (shadhdh), and the severely irregular report (munkar).

An important question Al-Khatib addresses is whether weak hadiths can be used at all. The majority position, which he articulates carefully, is that weak hadiths may be used in non-legal contexts — encouragements to virtue, warnings against vice, general moral instruction — provided they are not fabricated and provided one does not claim they are authentic. This is the basis for the widespread scholarly practice of citing weak hadiths in books of spirituality and ethics while being more restrictive in legal works. Al-Khatib's careful distinctions here established the parameters for a debate that continued throughout the classical period.
title_id: Kategori Hadis: Sahih, Hasan, dan Dha'if
content_id:
Al-Kifayah fi Ilm al-Riwayah mengakhiri pembahasan sistematisnya tentang transmisi hadis dengan analisis tentang bagaimana karakteristik individual para perawi dan sanad-sanad mereka bergabung untuk menghasilkan kategori-kategori hadis yang berbeda. Klasifikasi hadis sebagai sahih, hasan, atau dha'if bukan sekadar label teknis — ia merupakan penilaian akhir tentang apakah sebuah hadis dapat dijadikan hujjah dalam hukum Islam dan akidah.

Al-Khatib mendefinisikan hadis sahih sebagai hadis yang sanadnya bersambung melalui perawi-perawi yang adil dan dhabit (tepat hafalannya), tanpa cacat (illah) atau kejanggalan (syudzudz). Beliau menjelaskan bahwa keshahihan sebuah hadis bukan ditentukan oleh satu kriteria tunggal, melainkan oleh kombinasi dari seluruh karakteristik sanad dan matannya.

Hadis hasan menempati posisi antara sahih dan dha'if. Al-Khatib mendiskusikan bagaimana al-Tirmidzi memperkenalkan istilah ini dan bagaimana para ulama berbeda pendapat tentang definisi pastinya. Pada umumnya hadis hasan adalah hadis yang memenuhi syarat-syarat hadis sahih tetapi ada salah satu perawinya yang tingkat dhabt-nya sedikit lebih rendah dari perawi hadis sahih.

Hadis dha'if mencakup semua hadis yang tidak memenuhi standar sahih atau hasan karena kelemahan dalam sanad atau matannya. Al-Khatib mengidentifikasi berbagai jenis kelemahan — mulai dari terputusnya sanad, perawi yang majhul (tidak dikenal), perawi yang dianggap tidak dapat dipercaya, hingga cacat tersembunyi dalam matan hadis itu sendiri.

Penutup kitab ini menegaskan bahwa ilmu riwayah pada akhirnya adalah ilmu yang melayani tujuan yang lebih besar: memastikan bahwa umat Islam dapat mengetahui dengan keyakinan apa yang sesungguhnya diriwayatkan dari Nabi ﷺ, agar sunnahnya dapat diikuti dengan benar dan warisan kenabiannya terpelihara sepanjang zaman.
